text: This is the case of a man in his late seventies who'd had multiple previous operations including a splenectomy, an operation for a perforated duodenal ulcer, a cholecystectomy and a right colectomy for cancer all performed through a long midline incision. He'd been identified as having a lesion in the chordate lobe of his liver presumed to be on the basis of NASH cirrhosis. It had been biopsied and confirmed to be hepatocellular carcinoma.

The patient is positioned supine with legs split, and the surgeon stands between the legs. The falciform ligament is divided, followed by the division of the left triangular ligament, allowing retraction of the left lateral section and part of the left lobe of the liver towards the right side.

The surgeon divides the lesser omentum, focusing on the pars flaccida covering the chordate process and tumor. A Nathanson liver retractor is positioned beneath the left lateral section of the liver for enhanced exposure. The peritoneal reflections from the diaphragm to the lesser omentum are dissected, completing the opening of the pars flaccida. The golf ball-sized tumor is visible inferiorly in the chordate process, while the superior caudate process appears relatively normal.

The surgeon dissects and divides the ligamentum venosum near its insertion into the leptopatic vein, retracting the tissue inferiorly to resect the entire fat pad including the ligament. Adhesions near the portal origin of the ligamentum venosum are removed, and the ligament is divided using an energy device. The tissue is set aside for specimen retrieval. Fatty adhesions between the lesser omental fat and the chordate lobe are left attached to the tumor to maintain a clear margin. The hepatic artery is visible pulsing inferiorly in the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully navigates around the rounded hepatocellular carcinoma, avoiding excessive pressure on the tumor. The first view of the inferior vena cava and the chordate tributary is visible, with the chordate process bridge crossing behind the portohepatous and in front of the vena cava. The portal inflow to the chordate process is approached, and superficial tissue is divided to lengthen the area. A small branch is torn and managed with bipolar diathermy.

The surgeon uses an energy device to dissect and lengthen small vessels around the chordate process, avoiding clips to prevent clip traffic for later stapling. Extra peritoneal attachments to the superior portion of the chordate process are carefully dissected, gradually freeing the tumor on three of four sides, leaving a bridge of chordate parenchyma to staple through.

The surgeon successfully lengthens the tissue between the porta hepatis and the caudate process, exposing and dividing the bridge on the patient's left side. Small tributaries of the inferior vena cava are visible, with one vessel being clipped to control the annoying ooze. A suction device is used to manage the bleeding and maintain visibility in the surgical field.

The surgeon places a locking hemolock clip on the inferior vena cava side to prevent dislodgement by passing instruments. A gauze square is used to control bleeding as the approach to the supralateral aspect of the inferior vena cava continues. The retrocaval ligament is developed, revealing a lateral vessel at the ligament's start, which is challenging to access in open surgery but manageable laparoscopically. The chordate process is elevated despite its awkward shape.

The surgeon uses a retracting instrument to elevate the superior aspect of the chordate lobe anteriorly, preparing a landing zone for the stapler. The chordate lobe is densely held to the inferior vena cava through ligamentous wrappings and short hepatic veins, which are gradually dealt with. A short hepatic vein is identified, clipped, and divided using an energy device.

The surgeon carefully dissects areolar tissue in front of the inferior vena cava, avoiding avulsion of tributaries. The posterior superior corner of the caudate process is released, thinning down the pre-caval bridge behind the porta hepatis while controlling small oozing vessels. Additional inflow is taken to ensure adequate length for stapler passage, avoiding pinching the tumor.

The surgeon passes anteriorly over the left portal structures, thinning down the tissue to prevent the stapler from pulling in the tumor or unwanted structures. The assistant actively uses a sucker to manage minor venous oozing. The chordate process is lifted forward, stopping the bleeding, allowing the stapler to be positioned at the same angle as the sucker, ensuring it does not pinch the inferior vena cava.

The surgeon uses a stapler as a crushing device to compress and thin the parenchyma, allowing the advancement of the stapler with a 60mm vascular load. After compressing for 30 to 60 seconds, the stapler is fired. A second stapler is introduced to address the remaining small bridge of tissue in front of the inferior vena cava, ensuring the tumor is kept out of the staple line.

The surgeon sets aside the specimen after ensuring there is no massive bleeding. Fat from the chest ligament is placed with the specimen, and a bag is introduced for removal. The specimen is extracted through an incisional hernia site, avoiding pelvic adhesions. After cleaning, most of the oozing has stopped, and fibrillar is applied.

The surgeon uses a sucker to locate a vessel that has opened up, likely an arterial branch, and applies metallic leader clips to control the bleeding. Despite poor exposure, the clips are placed effectively, ensuring no further bleeding. Additional fibula is used to manage mesenteric ooze from adhesiolysis, and the abdomen is closed without a drain.
His pathology report showed a forty five millimeter HCC with a four millimeter surgical margin without vascular invasion and well differentiated
